source_metadata.title_raw: Gary S. Winuk - A-19-234 - January 14, 2020 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Gary S. Winuk 
Kaufman Legal Group 
A Professional Corporation 
621 Capitol Mall, Suite 1900 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-234 
Dear Mr. Winuk: 
January 14, 2020 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act ("Act") and Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 Please note that we 
are only providing advice under the Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of 
interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts 
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for 
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against 
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Under the Act and Section 1090, may Santa Monica City Councilmember Greg Morena's 
restaurant continue to rent real property from the City after its current lease expires and converts to 
a month-to-month tenancy? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-234 
Page No. 2 
Yes, because the lease specifically contemplated a potential month-to-month tenancy 
following the expiration of the lease's term, and that contract was created prior to Councilmember 
Morena taking office, continued rental payments would not involve the "making" of a contract, 
prohibited under Section 1090. However, under the Act, Councilmember Morena has an economic 
interest in The Albright and may not take part in decisions that would have a reasonably 
foreseeable, material effect on that business, including the City's potential decision of whether to 
terminate the month-to-month tenancy. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Greg Morena was elected to the Santa Monica City Council on November 6, 2018. He is a 
joint owner of The Albright- SM Pier Seafood, which operates a restaurant, the Albright, on the 
Santa Monica Pier. The Santa Monica Pier is owned by the City of Santa Monica and managed by 
the Santa Monica Pier Corporation (Pier Corporation). The Albright leases space on the Pier from 
the City through the Pier Corporation. The lease between the City and the Albright runs through 
June 2020. The lease agreement between the City and the Albright contains a section titled, 
"Holding Over," which provides: 
If Tenant shall be in possession of the Premises after the expiration of the Term of 
any extension or renewal thereof with the express consent of Landlord, then, in the 
absence of a written agreement extending the Term hereof, the tenancy under this 
Lease shall become, at Landlord's option, one from month to month, terminable by 
either party on thirty (30) days' prior written notice, at monthly rental equal to twice 
the sum of (i) the monthly installment of Annual Base Rent payable during the last 
month of the Term and (ii) one twelfth (1112th) of the average Annual Percentage 
Rate payable hereunder for the final three (3) Rental Years (defined in Section 5.4). 
Tenant shall also pay all other charges payable under the terms of the Lease, prorated 
for each month during which Tenant remains in possession. Such month-to-month 
tenancy shall also be subject to all other conditions, provisions and obligations of 
this Lease (except for those pertaining to the Term). Should Tenant hold over with 
Landlord's express written consent, Tenant shall indemnify, defend and hold 
Landlord harmless from and against any and all claims, demands, actions, losses, 
damages, expenses, costs and the like, including but not limited to Landlord's actual 
attorneys' fees and costs or the value of all legal services supplied to Landlord, 
suffered or incurred by Landlord by reason of Tenant's failure to timely surrender 
and vacate the Premises upon the expiration or earlier termination of this Lease. 
Tenant shall not interpose any counterclaims in a summary proceeding or other 
action based on holdover. 
You write, "[i]n other words, once the lease between the City and The Albright expires in June 
2020, the lease converts to a month-to-month lease under the same terms and conditions as the 
existing lease." As the terms above specify, however, the rental rate for the month-to-month 
tenancy would be substantially increased. 


ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
File No. A-19-234 
Page No. 3 
Under California Government Code Section 1090, public officials "shall not be financially 
interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which 
they are members." Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or 
minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided 
allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 
569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the 
appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) Typically, a contract is 
"made" on mutual assent of the involved parties. (Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 569.) In addition, 
making or participating in making a contract has been broadly construed to include those instances 
where a public official has influence over the contract or its terms. (See 80 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41.) 
A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The 
prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 
646-649.) 
Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law 
and Attorney General opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as 
direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of 
pecuniary gain. (People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847,867, fn. 5; Terry v. Bender (1956) 
143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 
161-162 (2001 ). ) Officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit 
from it in any way. (Honig, supra, 48 Cal.App.4th at p. 333.) 
In Winuk Advice Letter, No. A-18-275, you sought advice as to whether the Albright could 
continue and renew its pre-existing lease with the Pier Corporation under the Act and Section 1090. 
There, we advised that under Section 1090, "[t]he Albright may continue their pre-existing lease 
with the Santa Monica Pier Corporation, under the present terms and conditions of the lease until 
the lease expiration, as the mere continuation of the existing leases would not involve the making of 
any governmental decisions, and thus not implicate Section 1090." We further explained, however, 
that "unless Councilmember Morena divests himself of the proscribed financial interest, the City is 
prohibited from modifying or extending the existing leases, even if he abstains from participating in 
the decision." We also wrote, "[i]n light of our conclusion that Section 1090 prohibits the renewal 
or amendment of these leases, further advice under the Act concerning the renewal or amendment 
of the leases is not necessary," and therefore declined to provide advice with respect to the Act. 
In explaining our advice in Winuk Advice Letter, No. A-18-275, we found City of Imperial 
Beach v. Bailey, supra, 103. Cal.App.3d 191 and a California Attorney General Opinion (81 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 134 (1998)) to be particularly relevant. In Imperial Beach, the California Court 
of Appeal held that the exercise of an option to renew a lease between a city and a city 
councilmember constituted an impermissible "making" of a contract under Section 1090. (Imperial 
Beach, supra, 103 Cal.App.Jd at p. 197.) In the Attorney General Opinion, the Attorney General 


File No. A-19-234 
Page No. 4 
advised that the negotiation of rental rates and water fees would constitute the "making" of a 
contract and present, at the least, the appearance of a conflict of interest that Section 1090 prohibits. 
(81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 134.) 
Unlike the question presented in the Winuk Advice Letter, No. A-18-275, the present 
circumstances are not analogous to Imperial Beach or the aforementioned Attorney General 
Opinion. Here, the lease's "holdover" provision-which was contracted prior to Councilmember 
Morena taking office-already contracted the terms for a month-to-month tenancy and a substantial 
increase in rent at the end of the lease's fixed term. In other words, the terms of the lease expressly 
envisioned and contracted for a scenario in which The Albright would remain in possession of the 
property beyond the expiration of the lease's defined term and protected the City's interest from 
fluctuations in the rental market. Additionally, because the terms were specified in the initial lease, 
no negotiation of contract terms would be involved. 
Also significant, the substantial increase in rent provided by the existing lease further 
evidences that the existing lease will be continued without regard to Councilmember Moreno's 
status as an officeholder. This contractual term was negotiated prior to Councilmember Moreno 
taking office and distinguishes the continuation of the existing lease from a decision to extend a 
lease without a corresponding rent increase in a manner which the City may not have extended to 
any other renter. Accordingly, The Albright's payment of increased rent under the terms of the 
lease's "holdover" provision would not constitute a prohibited "making" of a contract under Section 
1090. 
The Act 
We note, however, that under the Act, Councilmember Morena is prohibited from taking 
part in decisions that would have a reasonably foreseeable, material effect on The Albright. 
Under Section 87100 of the Act, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." Section 87103 specifies that "[a] public official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a 
member or his or her immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests. Such 
economic interests include "( a ]ny business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(a).) 
Regulation 18701(a) provides the standard for determining whether a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable when an official's economic interest is explicitly involved in a governmental 
decision. It states: "[a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision 
before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6)." 


File No. A-19-234 
Page No. 5 
Regulation 18702.1 provides that "[t]he reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity is material if ... [t]he 
entity is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision, including any decision in which the entity 
... [b ]ids on, or enters into, a contract with the agency, or is identified as a subcontractor on a bid 
or contract with the agency .... " (Regulation 18702.l(a)(l)(C).) 
Although Regulation 18233 provides that a month-to-month tenancy does not constitute an 
"interest in real property" under the Act, Councilmember Morena still has a financial interest in The 
Albright as a business entity. Accordingly, under the Act, he is prohibited from taking part in 
decisions that would have a reasonably foreseeable, material effect on The Albright, including 
decisions that The Albright is the subject of. Specifically, if the City were to consider ending the 
month-to-month tenancy with The Albright in order to pursue a lease with another party, perhaps 
for the sake of securing a long-term lease or negotiating a different rental rate, Councilmember 
Morena would be prohibited from taking part in such a decision. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:sal 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Under the Act and Section 1090, may Santa Monica City Councilmember Greg Morena's 
restaurant continue to rent real property from the City after its current lease expires and converts to 
a month-to-month tenancy?

prohibited under Section 1090. However, under the Act, Councilmember Morena has an economic 
interest in The Albright and may not take part in decisions that would have a reasonably 
foreseeable, material effect on that business, including the City's potential decision of whether to 
terminate the month-to-month tenancy.

QUESTION: Under the Act and Section 1090, may Santa Monica City Councilmember Greg Morena's 
restaurant continue to rent real property from the City after its current lease expires and converts to 
a month-to-month tenancy?

CONCLUSION: Yes, because the lease specifically contemplated a potential month-to-month tenancy 
following the expiration of the lease's term, and that contract was created prior to Councilmember 
Morena taking office, continued rental payments would not involve the "making" of a contract, 
prohibited under Section 1090. However, under the Act, Councilmember Morena has an economic 
interest in The Albright and may not take part in decisions that would have a reasonably 
foreseeable, material effect on that business, including the City's potential decision of whether to 
terminate the month-to-month tenancy.
